content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 2, 2016

Robert Boco
Sr. Assistant City Attorney
456 W. Olive Avenue
P.O. Box 3707
Sunnyvale, CA 94088

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-16-058

Dear Mr. Boco:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

     May Economic Development Manager Connie Verceles, whose spouse is a mid-level manager at Apple Inc. ("Apple") in Cupertino, participate in governmental decisions that involve the permits and entitlements for Apple office locations and new development projects within the City of Sunnyvale?

CONCLUSION

     Because decisions regarding its offices and development projects require Apple to apply to the City directly for permits and entitlements, the interest of Ms. Verceles is explicitly involved in the decisions and the financial effect of the decisions on Apple is both foreseeable and material. Accordingly, she may not take part in the decisions involving Apple office sites and new development projects.

FACTS

     You are seeking this advice on behalf of Ms. Verceles. In her position as the Economic Development Manager for the City, Ms. Verceles is involved in two major areas during the development process. First, she participates in community engagement as the business liaison point

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-058
Page No. 2

person for the City by facilitating information between prospective business developers/tenants, the
community and various City departments and elected officials.

Second, she participates during the development entitlement process by submitting
comments to the City's project review committee for some of the larger office projects in
Sunnyvale, assisting with permits and often participating in development agreement negotiations
between the City and certain developers.

Apple is a fortune 500 company based in Cupertino, California. The company has been
gradually expanding its presence in Sunnyvale and is in the process of leasing approximately five to
eight new office sites as well as developing land owned by Apple. The company will apply to the
City directly for permits and entitlements for these projects. As an example, you stated that Apple
intends to make tenant improvements to at least two brand new office buildings that collectively
contain more than a million square feet of space. And Ms. Verceles might potentially be involved in
such things as expedited plan checks, inspections where she is asked to intervene if inspections take
too long.

The spouse of Ms. Verceles works at the Cupertino office location as a mid-level manager
and he currently has no plans to transfer to any of the new Sunnyvale site locations. They own some
shares in Apple stock but they do not own either a leasehold interest or property interest in any of
the proposed Apple locations in Sunnyvale.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from participating in governmental decisions in
which they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family or on one or more of the official's interests. The financial interests are:

1. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect²
investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more.

2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect
interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more.

3. Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending
institution made in the regular course of business on terms available to
the public without regard to official status, aggregating five hundred
dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by,
the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

2 "For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the
spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-
percent interest or greater." (Section 87103.)

File No. A-16-058
Page No. 3

4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.

5. Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or
gifts aggregating [$460] or more in value provided to, received by, or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

6. Personal finances, meaning the financial effect of a governmental
decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her
immediate family.

Ms. Verceles has a community property interest in her spouse's income from Apple that
presumably will equal or exceed $500 in the 12 months prior to a decision. Therefore, she has an
interest in Apple as a source of income. (Section 87103(c).) In addition, she has an interest in Apple
as business entity assuming her spouse's stock shares in the company are worth at least $2,000.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is
"explicitly involved" in the governmental decision. An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if
it is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest. (Ibid.) Likewise, any application to the
official's agency for a permit, license, variance, exception or other entitlement is considered a
material effect. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(5); see also Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).)

Because decisions regarding its offices and development projects require Apple to apply to
the City directly for permits and entitlements, the interest of Ms. Verceles is explicitly involved in
the decisions and the financial effect of the decisions on Apple is both foreseeable and material.
Accordingly, she may not make, participate, or use your official position to influence the decisions.³

³ Even though these decisions may not affect Apple's stock price as your letter suggests, based upon the magnitude of
the decisions, we do not view their effect as "nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant" under Regulation 18702(b).

File No. A-16-058
Page No. 4


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:jgl

sections.question:
May Economic Development Manager Connie Verceles, whose spouse is a mid-level 
manager at Apple Inc. ("Apple") in Cupertino, participate in governmental decisions that involve 
the permits and entitlements for Apple office locations and new development projects within the 
City of Sunnyvale?
sections.conclusion:
Because decisions regarding its offices and development projects require Apple to apply to 
the City directly for permits and entitlements, the interest of Ms. Verceles is explicitly involved in 
the decisions and the financial effect of the decisions on Apple is both foreseeable and material. 
Accordingly, she may not take part in the decisions involving Apple office sites and new 
development projects.

You are seeking this advice on behalf of Ms. Verceles. In her position as the Economic 
Development Manager for the City, Ms. Verceles is involved in two major areas during the 
development process. First, she participates in community engagement as the business liaison poinperson for the City by facilitating information between prospective business developers/tenants, the 
community and various City departments and elected officials. 
Second, she participates during the development entitlement process by submitting 
comments to the City's project review committee for some of the larger office projects in 
Sunnyvale, assisting with permits and often participating in development agreement negotiations 
between the City and certain developers. 
Apple is a fortune 500 company based in Cupertino, California. The company has been 
gradually expanding its presence in Sunnyvale and is in the process of leasing approximately five to 
eight new office sites as well as developing land owned by Apple. The company will apply to the 
City directly for permits and entitlements for these projects. As an example, you stated that Apple

contain more than a million square feet of space. And Ms. Verceles might potentially be involved in 
such things as expedited plan checks, inspections where she is asked to intervene if inspections take 
too long. 
The spouse of Ms. Verceles works at the Cupertino office location as a mid-level manager 
and he currently has no plans to transfer to any of the new Sunnyvale site locations. They own some 
shares in Apple stock but they do not own either a leasehold interest or property interest in any of 
the proposed Apple locations in Sunnyvale.

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from participating in governmental decisions in 
which they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial 
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family or on one or more of the official's interests. The financial interests are: 
1. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect2 
investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
3. Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending 
institution made in the regular course of business on terms available to 
the public without regard to official status, aggregating five hundred 
dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, 
the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
decision is made. 
2 "For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the 
spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust 
in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a IO­
percent interest or greater." (Section 87103.)  
4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
5. Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or 
gifts aggregating ($460] or more in value provided to, received by, or 
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when 
the decision is made.

decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her 
immediate family.

Because decisions regarding its offices and development projects require Apple to apply to

Accordingly, she may not make, participate, or use your official positon to influence the decisions.3 
3 Even though these decisions may not affect Apple's stock price as your letter suggests, based upon the magnitude of 
the decisions, we do not view their effect as "nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant" under Regulation 18702(b).

QUESTION: May Economic Development Manager Connie Verceles, whose spouse is a mid-level 
manager at Apple Inc. ("Apple") in Cupertino, participate in governmental decisions that involve 
the permits and entitlements for Apple office locations and new development projects within the 
City of Sunnyvale?

CONCLUSION: Because decisions regarding its offices and development projects require Apple to apply to 
the City directly for permits and entitlements, the interest of Ms. Verceles is explicitly involved in 
the decisions and the financial effect of the decisions on Apple is both foreseeable and material. 
Accordingly, she may not take part in the decisions involving Apple office sites and new 
development projects.
